response:
## Decision: Proceed — staged (Option B)

**Proceed with the routing experiment, but gate spend in two stages exactly as ISSUE-109.md already sketches**: first, cheaply measure checker false-accept rate on the existing Haiku-solo artifacts from `benchmarks/2026-07-10-tiered-execution/` (no new routing runs needed — the checker's job, judging a finished artifact, is decoupled from how it was produced). Only if a checker variant clears ~10–15% false-accept does the full cost/quality grid run. Before that second-stage spend, finalize and pre-register the adopt rule per ADR 0024 (non-inferiority margin -0.05 on clustered CI, cost bar) **and** add a direct false-accept-rate gate to that rule — not just aggregate frac-met — so a checker that clears the mean bar by having correctly-escalated cells pull the average up cannot still ship a ~25%-false-accept config. Run at least one checker variant that isn't a same-family Sonnet judge (e.g. a checklist-scored deterministic check) to avoid probe #2's correlated-blind-spot risk.

## Why not A or C

- **Not C (decline).** ADR 0035 rejected *coach-and-retry* tiering (weak implementer iterating against a strong critic converges to the implementer's ceiling) — a mechanistically distinct claim from routing's (quality floor = whichever model ships, not Haiku's revision ability). Declining without measuring this shape is exactly what ADR 0024's rejected alternatives forbid: "keep [or reject] artifacts on assertion, no measurement." ADR 0035 itself only closed per-task orchestration at this granularity, not routing.
- **Not A unstaged (full grid immediately).** The issue's own text already conditions the grid on a checker fidelity screen ("If no checker beats ~10-15% false-accept... the null should be recorded without a full grid"). #41 already measured a Sonnet validator at 25% false-accept on this exact task family (ADR 0035:27). Running the full hermetic grid before checking whether any checker variant clears the bar spends the expensive half of the experiment on a very plausible predetermined null.

## Load-bearing assumption

Checker false-accept rate is a property of the (checker, finished-artifact) pair, measurable standalone and cheaply on held-out artifacts, and that number is predictive of the checker's behavior inside the live routing loop — i.e., false-accept isn't an emergent property that only manifests once the full escalation pipeline runs (e.g., because the loop selectively routes the checker toward exactly the marginal Haiku outputs that are hardest to judge correctly, a selection effect a held-out sample wouldn't reproduce).

## Failure class accepted

If that assumption is false, the cheap screen gives a false signal in either direction:
- **False green** — screen says a variant clears 10–15%, but in the live loop (selection-effect-correlated with premortem #2's same-family blind spot on judgment-heavy tasks) it doesn't. This is the dangerous direction: it authorizes the full grid, and if the grid's own decision rule isn't pinned to a direct false-accept gate (the mitigation above), a "proceed" verdict could still ship a config with a materially higher real-world false-accept rate than what was measured — the intermittent quality-cliff-that-looked-clean-in-CI failure premortem #1 describes.
- **False red** — screen kills a variant that would have worked in the full loop, foreclosing a viable config on an unrepresentative held-out sample.

I'm accepting the false-green branch as the bound: the direct false-accept gate on the *full-grid* decision rule (not just the cheap screen) is the actual backstop against shipping a silently-degraded config — the screen only controls spend, it is not itself the adopt gate.
